original_text: The United States implements a national carbon tax to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: least_preferable
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax set at a negligible rate, riddled with broad industry exemptions and lacking enforcement mechanisms, failing to meaningfully reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: low
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax set at a low fixed rate with limited sectoral coverage and weak compliance provisions, achieving only marginal reductions in greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: below_midpoint
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax at a moderate rate but without a clear escalation schedule or revenue recycling strategy, producing below-target reductions in greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: midpoint
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: above_midpoint
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax with an evidence-based price trajectory and broad sectoral coverage, paired with targeted rebates for low-income households, to effectively reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: high
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax with a robust, annually escalating price schedule, comprehensive sectoral coverage, border carbon adjustments, and progressive revenue recycling, achieving substantial reductions in greenhouse gas emissions.

variations.label: most_preferable
variations.text: The United States implements a national carbon tax informed by the latest climate science, featuring an optimal price trajectory, universal sectoral coverage, border carbon adjustments, progressive dividend returns, and adaptive review mechanisms, maximizing greenhouse gas emission reductions while ensuring economic efficiency and social equity.

quality_checks.reasoning: All tiers describe the same fundamental scenario—a US national carbon tax—while varying the sophistication, comprehensiveness, and effectiveness of the policy design. Each tier adds meaningful design features (escalation schedules, sectoral coverage, border adjustments, equity provisions, adaptive mechanisms) that make the policy strictly more effective and preferable than the prior tier.

preference_direction: Tier 7 is most preferable because a carbon tax that is scientifically grounded, optimally priced, universally applied, protected against carbon leakage, equitable in revenue distribution, and adaptively managed will maximize emission reductions while minimizing economic harm and social inequity—the best possible version of this policy.
